title: Radical Feminism
author: Mackay

opening_move.description: Feminist activism has seen an apparent resurgence in the UK since the early 2000s — larger marches, more mainstream visibility — yet Mackay's research on the British Women's Liberation Movement finds that motivations remain unchanged from the Second Wave (sexual harassment, male violence, discrimination) while methods have professionalized and women-only space has declined.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Comparing Second Wave and contemporary feminist activists reveals continuity in drivers (male violence, discrimination) but significant shifts in form (professionalization, inclusion of men, decline of separatism) — suggesting the resurgence is shaped by neoliberal conditions that constrain feminist organizing.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper presents empirical findings from a mixed-methods study of British feminist activists, comparing Second and Third Wave. The implicit argument is that apparent progress (resurgence, mainstream visibility) coexists with structural continuity (same harms) and organizational regression (professionalization at cost of DIY radicalism).

epistemic_mode.quantitative_methods: Survey of over 100 feminist activists (different schools: socialist, Christian anarchist, radical lesbian)
epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Interviews with feminist activists across generations; participant observation in the British Women's Liberation Movement; comparative analysis of Second Wave vs. contemporary organizing.
data_sources: 100+ survey respondents across UK feminist movement
interviews with Second Wave and contemporary activists
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Comparative movement sociology; qualitative thematic analysis; feminist standpoint methodology.
epistemic_mode.notes: This is a short TCS 'website highlights' piece (book note format, ~4 pages) summarizing findings from Mackay's book 'Radical Feminism: Feminist Activism in Movement' (2015). It is primarily empirical qualitative in character. The format is unusual: it's a compressed research summary rather than a full article.

case_type.case_name: British Women's Liberation Movement (Second Wave and contemporary resurgence)

confidence_notes: This is a very short book-note/research-summary format piece (TCS 'Website Highlights' section). The paper is primarily empirical qualitative, based on survey and interview data from 100+ UK feminist activists. The normative stake is implicit: the contrast between visible resurgence and persistent violence motivations, set against a 'neoliberal backdrop', implies a critique of professionalized, de-radicalized feminist organizing.
